confidence.disclosure: Dhul-Nun al-Misri (Thawban ibn Ibrahim) is one of the founding figures of classical Sufism, but the historical record about his life is sparse and heavily overlaid with legend (alchemy, conversation with animals, knowledge of hieroglyphs). The dates of his birth in Akhmim, his trial in Baghdad before the caliph al-Mutawakkil, and his death in Giza are reasonably firm; the rest is largely undated. The journey reflects this: a stationary life in Upper Egypt, a brief and dramatic visit to Baghdad, and return to die in Egypt.

journey.event: Born around 796 (180 AH) in Akhmim in Upper Egypt, an ancient city on the Nile with a strong Coptic Christian community and ruins of pharaonic temples. His father is a Nubian or Berber freedman.

As a young man studies hadith and the religious sciences in Egypt. Later legend (probably reflecting the strong tradition of Hellenistic-Coptic learning in Akhmim) credits him with knowledge of alchemy, medicine, ancient Egyptian hieroglyphs, and the secrets of the natural world.

Travels north to Fustat (Old Cairo), the capital of Muslim Egypt, and becomes a student of the Maliki jurist and ascetic Imam Malik's pupils there. Studies hadith with Egyptian scholars including al-Layth ibn Sa'd's circle.

Performs hajj and travels in the Hijaz. Meets and learns from a number of early Sufis. He is the first major figure to be associated explicitly with the science of the spiritual states (ahwal) and stations (maqamat) - the framework that will become central to all later Sufism.

Returns to Upper Egypt and lives mostly in Akhmim and the surrounding desert. Earns a reputation for intense asceticism, visions, and ecstatic poetry. Many of the early sayings about ma'rifa (gnosis) are attributed to him.

Comes under suspicion from the more literalist scholars of Egypt, who accuse him of teaching innovations and even zandaqa (heresy). His ecstatic utterances and his alleged claims to special knowledge from God are reported to the authorities.

Around 850 is summoned to Baghdad to be examined by the caliph al-Mutawakkil. Travels north under guard. In the caliph's presence speaks with such eloquence and humility about the love of God that al-Mutawakkil is moved to tears and releases him with honour.

Spends some time in Baghdad after his vindication. Meets and influences members of the developing Iraqi Sufi school: men like al-Sari al-Saqati, who is the teacher of al-Junayd. Through these contacts the Egyptian and Iraqi traditions of mysticism are linked.

Returns to Egypt and resumes his ascetic life in Upper Egypt. His circle of disciples includes both ordinary Muslims and other ascetics. The famous early Sufi prayers attributed to him date from this period.

Dies around 859 (245 AH) at Giza, near the pyramids on the west bank of the Nile opposite Fustat. He is buried in the small cemetery of the Sufis at the Qarafa in Cairo, where his tomb is venerated to the present day. His ecstatic prayers are still recited by Sufis around the Muslim world.
journey.source: Ibn Khallikan, Wafayat; Encyclopaedia of Islam
